title: Lesson 8: The Grace Cycle to Forgiveness
description: Four words explain what God did for you at the cross: Mercy, Justification, Righteousness, and Grace. Through an interactive diagram you will build piece by piece, you will see how these four realities work together in a cycle that starts with God's love and flows through you to others.

blocks.text: In the last lesson, you walked through the one storyline that holds the entire Bible together, from creation to new creation. You saw that every page points to Jesus. Now we are going to zoom in on what Jesus actually accomplished for you at the cross. This lesson is built around four words that every Christian should know: Mercy, Justification, Righteousness, and Grace. These are big church words, but they describe something so simple and so good that it will change the way you see God, yourself, and the people around you.
Why This Lesson Matters

Most people know that Jesus died for their sins. But if you ask them what that actually means, the answer gets fuzzy fast. This lesson takes away the fog. When you understand mercy, justification, righteousness, and grace, you do not just know more theology. You know what God thinks about you right now. You know why your standing before Him is secure. And you know why the Christian life is not about performing to earn approval but about receiving a gift and giving it away.

Here is the big idea: God started the Grace Cycle toward you. You receive it. And now you give it away through forgiveness, mercy, and good works. That is the rhythm of the Christian life. Let's see how it works.
Before we open the diagram, read one passage that contains all four ideas in just a few verses. This will be our guide for the rest of the lesson.

But when the goodness and loving kindness of God our Savior appeared, he saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy, by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit, whom he poured out on us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior, so that being justified by his grace we might become heirs according to the hope of eternal life.

Did you catch it? Mercy. Justified. Grace. Righteousness. All in one sentence. Paul is describing the biggest thing that has ever happened to you, and he packs it into a few lines. The diagram below will unpack it slowly, one piece at a time.
The Cycle of Grace

This interactive tool walks you through each of the four concepts one step at a time. Tap Begin to start, then use Next to move through each stop. Watch how the arrows connect, and notice what appears in the center when you reach the final step. Take your time. Read each verse card carefully before moving on.
8.1 - After walking through the diagram, which of the four concepts (Mercy, Justified, Righteous, Grace) surprised you or hit you the hardest? Why?
Grace and Mercy: Two Sides of God's Love

Look at the top two boxes of the diagram. Grace sits on the left. Mercy sits on the right. They are paired because they describe two sides of the same love. Mercy is not getting the punishment you do deserve. You broke the law, you earned the sentence, but the Judge does not bring the hammer down. Grace is getting a gift you do not deserve. Not only does the Judge let you go, He takes you home and gives you everything His own Son has.

Some people picture God as reluctant to forgive, as though you have to beg and plead before He finally agrees to show mercy. That is not the God of the Bible. Scripture says He is slow to anger and overflowing with steadfast love. His anger has a safety lock, but His mercy has a hair trigger. He loves to show mercy. It is what He delights to do.
The Lord, the Lord, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness, keeping steadfast love for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin.

Here is the simplest way to remember the difference. Think of a courtroom. You committed the crime. The evidence is clear. The Just Judge decides you do not have to pay the fine or do the time. That is mercy. Then the same Judge buys you a house and a car because He loves you so much. That is grace. Mercy takes away what you earned. Grace gives you what you never could.
Justified and Righteous: Your New Standing

Now look at the bottom two boxes. Justified sits on the right. Righteous sits on the left. These two describe what happens to your record before God. Justified means your punishment has been paid. Think of it as "just-if-I'd" never sinned. Jesus stepped into the courtroom, took your sentence, and satisfied the court on your behalf. The debt is gone. Righteous means you are now seen as perfect before God. Not because you became perfect overnight, but because God took the perfect, spotless life that Jesus lived and placed it on your account.
Theologians call this The Great Exchange. On the cross, God took your sin and placed it on Jesus. And He took the perfect record that Jesus earned and placed it on you.
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.

Read that again slowly. He became sin for you so that you could become the righteousness of God. Not just forgiven. Not just pardoned with a blank record. Clothed in the full righteousness of Christ. Your standing before God is not a patched-up version of the old one. It is brand new.
8.2 - What does it mean to you that Jesus did not just clear your record, but placed His own perfect record on your account? How does that change the way you see yourself?
How the Pairs Connect

The diagram is not just four separate ideas. It is a cycle, and the pairs are connected. Look at the right side of the diagram: Mercy flows down to Justification. Mercy is the decision not to punish you. Justification is how that mercy becomes possible, because Jesus stepped in and paid the price. Look at the left side: Righteousness flows up to Grace. Justification produced a new standing (righteousness), and that standing is wrapped in the extravagant, unearned generosity of God (grace).

And right in the center of all four boxes, the cross holds everything in place. The cross is where mercy and justice met. The cross is where your old record was exchanged for a new one. The cross is where grace became possible. Every arrow in the diagram passes through the shadow of the cross.
The Cycle Comes Back Around

There is one more piece of the diagram that changes everything. Look at what happens when the arrow reaches Grace in the top left. It does not stop. It curves back across the top toward Mercy. Why? Because those who have received mercy become people who show mercy. Those who have received grace become people who live grace. The gift was never meant to stop with you.
Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you.

The whole Christian life is right there in that cycle. God's grace flows down through the cross, through mercy, through justification, through righteousness, and into your life. And then it flows back out of you toward others. Freely you have received. Freely give.

For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast. For we are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do.

Notice that Paul does not stop at "saved by grace." He keeps going. You are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works. Grace does not make you passive. Grace positions you. God saved you by grace so that you would become a channel of grace to the people around you. The cycle keeps turning: received, lived, given away.
8.3 - Who in your life needs you to show them the mercy or grace you have received from God? What would that look like this week?
Grace Changes the Reason You Fight

When people first hear about grace, they almost always ask the same question: if God has already forgiven everything, then why would anyone try to live a good life? Here is the answer: most belief systems say, "Do this and you will be accepted." The gospel flips that entirely. You are accepted now, so you can do. When you know your standing before God is secure, you do not become lazy. You become free. Free to obey out of love instead of fear. Free to fight sin from a place of security instead of fighting to earn a security you already have.

Remember what we said in Lesson 1: we are not fighting sin to have a relationship with God. We are fighting sin because we have a relationship with God. Grace does not end the fight against sin. It changes the reason you fight.
There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.

No condemnation. Not a little. Not some held in reserve. None. Because Jesus took your punishment (mercy and justification) and gave you His perfection (righteousness and grace), you stand before God as a fully accepted, fully loved child.

8.4 - Romans 8:1 says there is 'no condemnation' for those in Christ Jesus. In what part of your daily life do you most struggle to believe that? What would be different if you really let that truth stand?
This Week's Practice

This week, spend time each day with one part of the diagram. Day one, sit with Mercy. Day two, Justification. Day three, Righteousness. Day four, Grace. Day five, the Cross at the center. On the remaining days, look at the whole cycle and let it remind you that the grace you received is meant to flow through you. Every time you feel the pull to perform for God's approval, pause and say out loud: "I am justified. The sentence has been served. Jesus paid it all."

Also this week, read Romans 5:1-11. Notice how many good things Paul says flow from justification: peace with God, access to grace, hope, love. These are not things you earn after justification. They are things that come with it.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
